perturbation.original_text:
Section 3.2 Servicing of Receivables.
(a) General Servicing Obligations. The Servicer will manage, service, administer and collect on the Receivables with reasonable care using that degree of skill and attention that the Servicer exercises for all comparable device payment plan agreement receivables that it services for itself or others according to the Servicing Procedures. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the Servicer's obligations will include: (i) collecting and applying all payments made on, or credits applied to, the Receivables and any other amounts received related to the Depositor Transferred Property; (ii) investigating delinquencies; (iii) sending invoices and notices and responding to inquiries of Obligors; (iv) processing requests for extensions, modifications and adjustments; (v) administering payoffs, prepayments, defaults and delinquencies; (vi) maintaining accurate and complete accounts and receivables systems for servicing the Receivables; (vii) providing to the Custodian copies, or access to, any documents that modify or supplement information in the Receivable Files; and (viii) preparing and providing Monthly Investor Reports and any other periodic reports required to be prepared by the Servicer under this Agreement or any other Transaction Document.
perturbation.changed_text:
Section 3.2 Servicing of Receivables.
(a) General Servicing Obligations. The Servicer will manage, service, administer and collect on the Receivables with minimal effort using that degree of skill and attention that the Servicer deems necessary. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the Servicer's obligations will include: (i) collecting payments when feasible on the Receivables and any other amounts received related to the Depositor Transferred Property; (ii) optional investigation of delinquencies; (iii) sending invoices at its discretion and responding to inquiries of Obligors as it sees fit; (iv) processing requests for extensions, modifications and adjustments as it is able; (v) administering payoffs, prepayments, defaults and delinquencies as needed; (vi) maintaining accounts and receivables systems for servicing the Receivables as long as practical; (vii) providing the Custodian copies, or access to, any documents that modify or supplement information in the Receivable Files when it is convenient; and (viii) preparing and providing Monthly Investor Reports and any other periodic reports required to be prepared by the Servicer under this Agreement or any other Transaction Document only if time permits.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a certain level of care and specific obligations. The modified text significantly reduces the Servicer's obligations to a discretionary level, undermining the original intent of the agreement and creating uncertainty about the standard of care required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 12 CFR § 1024.38 - General servicing policies, procedures, and requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the law by not requiring the servicer to fulfill its duties. 12 CFR § 1024.38 details specific requirements a servicer must meet, such as responding to borrower inquiries and managing accounts accurately. The contract language changes these mandatory actions to optional ones.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the servicer's obligations, transforming mandatory actions into discretionary ones. This contradicts the law explanation, which states that 12 CFR § 1024.38 requires servicers to fulfill their duties. The modified contract makes critical servicing tasks optional, such as investigating delinquencies, sending invoices, and maintaining accurate accounts. The scraped law snippet refers to eCFR :: 12 CFR 1024.38, suggesting a federal regulation outlining general servicing policies, procedures, and requirements, which the modified text appears to undermine by making these obligations optional or subject to minimal effort. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text:
Section 4.2 Investment of Funds in Bank Accounts.
(a) Permitted Investments. If (i) no Default or Event of Default has occurred and is continuing and (ii) Cellco is the Servicer, the Servicer may instruct the Indenture Trustee to invest any funds in the Collection Account, the Acquisition Account, the Reserve Account and the Negative Carry Account in Permitted Investments and, if investment instructions are received, the Indenture Trustee will direct the Qualified Institution maintaining the Bank Accounts to invest the funds in the Collection Account, the Acquisition Account, the Reserve Account or the Negative Carry Account, as applicable, in those Permitted Investments; provided, that, if on any Payment Date, the amount on deposit in the Acquisition Account (after giving effect to the acquisition of any Additional Receivables on such date) is greater than 25% of the aggregate Note Balance (after giving effect to any payments made on the Notes on such date), the Servicer shall instruct the Indenture Trustee to invest any amounts in the Acquisition Account in excess of such amount in any Permitted Investments, other than (x) any investments set forth in clauses (b) or (c) of the definition of Permitted Investments that are held by or at the Indenture Trustee or (y) any investments set forth in clause (e) of the definition thereof.
perturbation.changed_text:
Section 4.2 Investment of Funds in Bank Accounts.
(a) Investment at Will. If (i) a Default or Event of Default has occurred and is continuing and (ii) Cellco is not the Servicer, the Servicer may instruct the Indenture Trustee to invest any funds in the Collection Account, the Acquisition Account, the Reserve Account and the Negative Carry Account in Non-Permitted Investments and, if investment instructions are received, the Indenture Trustee will direct the Qualified Institution maintaining the Bank Accounts to invest the funds in the Collection Account, the Acquisition Account, the Reserve Account or the Negative Carry Account, as applicable, in those Non-Permitted Investments; provided, that, if on any Payment Date, the amount on deposit in the Acquisition Account (after giving effect to the acquisition of any Additional Receivables on such date) is greater than 5% of the aggregate Note Balance (after giving effect to any payments made on the Notes on such date), the Servicer shall instruct the Indenture Trustee to invest any amounts in the Acquisition Account in excess of such amount in risky investments.
perturbation.explanation: The original text restricts investments to 'Permitted Investments' under certain conditions, with specific limitations. The modified version allows investments in 'Non-Permitted Investments' even during a default, and mandates investment in 'risky investments', contradicting prudent investment standards expected in such agreements and the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 12 CFR § 9.18 which outlines the regulations for collective investment funds managed by national banks. By allowing investments in 'Non-Permitted Investments' especially during default, and directing investment into 'risky investments', the modification violates the regulation's requirement for prudent management and investment policies.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows for 'Non-Permitted Investments' and directs investment into 'risky investments', particularly during a default. The Law Explanation states this contradicts 12 CFR § 9.18, which mandates prudent management and investment policies for collective investment funds managed by national banks. The original text restricts investments to 'Permitted Investments' under certain conditions. The modification removes this restriction and introduces 'risky investments', creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section 10.5 GOVERNING LAW. THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING THE RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF THE PARTIES HERETO, SHALL BE GOVERNED BY, AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH, THE INTERNAL LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK (INCLUDING SECTIONS 5-1401 AND 5-1402 OF THE GENERAL OBLIGATIONS LAW OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK, BUT WITHOUT REGARD TO ANY OTHERWISE APPLICABLE CONFLICTS OF LAW PRINCIPLES). FOR PURPOSES OF THE UCC, NEW YORK SHALL BE DEEMED TO BE THE SECURITIES INTERMEDIARY'S JURISDICTION, AND THE LAW OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK SHALL GOVERN ALL ISSUES SPECIFIED IN ARTICLE 2(1) OF THE HAGUE SECURITIES CONVENTION. NOTWITHSTANDING SECTION 10.1 OF THIS AGREEMENT, THE PARTIES WILL NOT AGREE TO AMEND THIS AGREEMENT TO CHANGE THE GOVERNING LAW TO ANY LAW OTHER THAN THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 10.5 GOVERNING LAW. THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING THE RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF THE PARTIES HERETO, SHALL BE GOVERNED BY, AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH, THE LAWS OF THE CAYMAN ISLANDS (WITHOUT REGARD TO ANY OTHERWISE APPLICABLE CONFLICTS OF LAW PRINCIPLES). FOR PURPOSES OF THE UCC, THE LOCATION OF THE INDENTURE TRUSTEE SHALL BE DEEMED TO BE THE JURISDICTION, AND THE LAW OF THAT LOCATION SHALL GOVERN ALL ISSUES SPECIFIED IN ARTICLE 2(1) OF THE HAGUE SECURITIES CONVENTION. NOTWITHSTANDING SECTION 10.1 OF THIS AGREEMENT, THE PARTIES WILL AGREE TO AMEND THIS AGREEMENT TO CHANGE THE GOVERNING LAW TO ANY LAW OTHER THAN THE LAWS OF THE CAYMAN ISLANDS.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies New York law as the governing law. The modified text changes this to the laws of the Cayman Islands. This could create conflict in enforcement if the assets or operations are primarily in the U.S., and Cayman Islands law might lack specific provisions applicable to this type of securitization.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles in UCC Article 1 and Conflicts of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to the Cayman Islands without demonstrating a reasonable relation to that jurisdiction directly contradicts the principle of "reasonable relation" as required by conflict of law principles and also the UCC. This creates uncertainty about which jurisdiction’s laws properly apply to the agreement, potentially making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from New York to the Cayman Islands directly contradicts the principle of 'reasonable relation'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section (a), states that 'when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties'. The altered contract fails to demonstrate how the Cayman Islands have a reasonable relation to the transaction, especially when the original text designates New York, likely indicating a prior established relationship. This creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating established conflict of law principles within the UCC, making the contract unenforceable. The Misaligned Terminology definition states that a legal contradiction arises when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. Here the changed text deviates from the UCC's reasonable relation requirement.
